speaker: interviewer
text: The 21st of July, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about your arrival in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's-- in a sense, I suppose, that is the end of the first chapter, because that's the death of my sister. That was, as I said, on the 7th of July, and she died on that day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were taken, then, to the bath house, if you can call it that. And we didn't know, of course, that we were running the risk of not being-- that water being not water but gas. But we didn't know that, so we accepted the fact we're going to have a shower.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before we had a shower-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --there were-- I can't tell you how many, because I would tell a huge lie-- but lots and lots and lots of German SS soldiers, lots and lots of Jewish workers standing around-- men, of course. By this time we were all women-- we were women, but there were all these men around. And they were we were told to strip. We said, strip? But there are people!

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, the answer to that would have been a huge whack on the head or whatever. So we had to strip. So we strip. Now, I was unfortunate enough to be immediately sexually assaulted. When I say sexually assaulted, don't think that that means an actual sexual intercourse, because no. But I was assaulted by hand. And again, I have never, ever forgotten the feeling of this person touching me [INAUDIBLE] obviously, never been touched. But having said that, in the end, that wasn't the most important. In the end, that somehow went into the background. The other things that happened had taken over. But I just thought I'd mention it, because it happened, so I thought I'd mention it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a German, or a--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, a German. A German, yes. A German. A young German soldier. In fact, if I remember correctly, it wasn't an SS, either. I think it was a soldier, a German soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other woman who were assaulted?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I'm sure there were. But I don't know because I've never talked-- in fact, to tell you the honest truth, I have never talked about it. I have never-- I don't think I've ever mentioned it to anyone, really. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, I don't think I have. But there it is. Anyway, so we had to-- we were on-- we were standing around there naked, and the next thing that happened was that we were shaven. Now, once hair was off, and I was looking for my sister, and I couldn't see her. The fact that she was standing right next to me, I didn't recognize her-- absolutely off. Then we were-- they poured some dreadful antiseptic on us. I immediately got awful sores on my on my scalp, because it just ate into my skin. And we were shaved everywhere-- pubic hair and underarms and everywhere. And the same stuff went on everywhere, and it was just so painful, I-- anyway. And then we were taken into the showers. So we had a shower, and then we were given clothes. Well, my sister and I, we must have been one of the last ones, I guess, to get some clothes, because all that was left for us were petticoats. So we lived our life in a petticoat, which came just far enough down to cover us, just. You couldn't bend-- just. We had no coat, we had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Auschwitz during the day in July is incredibly hot. Did you have underpants?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. That's why I said we couldn't bend. Nothing. We were given a petticoat. That's it. And we had some sort of clog. We didn't have shoes-- some sort of clog that we were clogging around. But you learned to walk in those things. But one's got to remember that Auschwitz at night in July is bitterly cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, our [INAUDIBLE] appels started at 4:00 AM, so we were herded out at 4 o'clock in the morning in that petticoat, and stood there for God knows how long-- four hours, whatever, just in that petticoat. And you know, we never got sick. Isn't that amazing? We never got sick. Was that every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, every morning. Every morning and every afternoon for about four or five hours, whatever, because they counted you. Anyway, again, I'm jumping ahead. So we had these petticoats, and then from there, we were marched into the actual compound. How long that march was, I have absolutely no idea. One thing I cannot remember, and that's the geography. I remember the geography of the camp I was in which, was C Lager-- C Lager, Lager C. But that's the only geography I remember. I cannot put together the rest of the camp, because you weren't allowed out of your lager, naturally. And the once or twice when they took you somewhere, you went, and you didn't know where you went. So anyhow, I'm no good on the geography. Anyway, we were taken into this this C Lager, and the barrack that we were taken to was, to the best of my remembrance-- and I'd just about stake my life on that-- was number 12. Now, that was a barrack for young women. We were all pretty young.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what shall I say to you? There were the beds-- did I say beds?-- were on top of each other. Of course, it would have been, what, about that much in between? So those who were lower down, I don't know how they didn't suffocate.

speaker: interviewee
text: As it happens, we were-- we were both-- well, we were on top. At least that's how I remembered that we were-- yeah, we must have been, because I remember looking out on the side slits. But you had these wooden bunks, everything. And there was no cover. There was nothing, absolutely nothing. Now, on each of them, there were about six to eight of us, and, of course, all you could do is lie-- lie on your side. Now, if one turned, then the whole lot had to turn. It was just-- it was truly like herrings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we arrived there, the first thing we did was to ask where our sister is. Where is she? So someone, and it was one of the Jewish girls there, said your sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that again? She said, [POINTING] can't you smell it? Well, of course we could, only we didn't know what it was. The crematorium-- I'm not saying that my sister was, by then, dead. We wouldn't know that. But the crematorium was-- well, in those days, fully at work all the time, because transports were coming in constantly. Hungarian transports were one of the biggest in speed, one after another. And it was just going, blazing away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you could smell this unbelievable smell, which never goes out of your nostrils ever. In fact, I smelled it once here in Australia, and that was at a cremation of a friend of mine where something was wrong. I know something was wrong with the crematorium, because I walked in there, and the next thing, I found myself on the floor. And I didn't know why. I fainted. And when I came to, I could smell the smell.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one believed me. They said I was hysterical. Hysterical? Anyhow, that was much later. So this lady let us know that my sister is dead. Well, we nearly went out of our minds. But we didn't believe her. But all the same, we nearly went out of our minds.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next morning, first morning in Auschwitz, we were, of course, herded out at 4 o'clock. We stood in line as usual. We were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --well, not as usual, because it was the first one. We were just standing. And suddenly, I-- well, not I, all of us-- we saw a young woman being dragged on both sides by two SS. She was on her knees, and she was dragged in front of us, as I said, on her knees.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of the SS had some sort of a wooden plank in his hand. The other had his-- his gun. And this girl right in front of us was beaten to death. And that was our first [INAUDIBLE] appel. Well, what can I say? Was any reason given?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We have no idea. No idea why or what. But she was-- I actually saw that girl's face disappear. She couldn't stand anymore. As I said, she was on her knees. And I actually saw that face, second by second, disappear. The eyes were gone, the blood everywhere, the mouth was gone. And, of course, she died, I would imagine, there and then, although I'm not absolutely sure. But I would think she would have had to. She was left there, lying.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wish I could tell you, but I can't. What can one say? How would-- how would one explain-- try and explain that unbelievable horror of-- and even then, it was the first morning, and even then, virtually a week-- not virtually, actually a week ago, of course, we were still in the ghetto. And in the ghetto we were still, as I said earlier, still human beings. So I-- to put it in a sort of peculiar way, we still had the smell of home, if you know what I mean. We still-- we were still human beings at that stage, because later on, we weren't, although I will say, for myself and also my sister, we remained human beings. But generally speaking, you still felt that you were a human being.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then suddenly you find yourself somewhere where the fence is electrified. The gates, of course, are electrified and shut, where you see these turrets with machine guns trained on you from all around, everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you see a person beaten to death in front of you. And you think, where am I? Where have they brought me? What's happening here? And what is this? What is it? And it slowly-- and I do remember thinking this clearly-- how will I ever get out? How will those gates actually open up? Because I knew that to get out of here, those gates actually have to open. So who's going to open it? When is it going to be open? Will I be around? Will any of us be around? And to know that the war had to end-- now, for the war to end, that's a myth, a story, something that is in books, where you say these things, that the war will end. But will it ever end? Will it ever end? Of course it won't ever end. Things like-- what I'm trying to say is that something incredibly positive had to happen, which was entirely out of your hands. You could not make anything happen. It had to happen through fate. How is it ever going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the realization again and again and again that you are in the hands of these-- I won't say beasts, because I don't want to hurt the animals-- of these things, these things who've got power over you 100%. And there you are in your-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --short cover-up, at their mercy, as they want it. I can't-- I've never been able to explain what it's like, and I believe that nobody can. But I suppose the difference, in a sense, in a sense-- the difference between talking about it and listening to it and it happening would be if you see an incredibly well-painted picture where, let's say fire-- you look at it and you think, this is real. But you touch it, and then you put your hand in the fire, in the real fire, and you'll know the difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one cannot-- it is not possible, because the suffering is so-- it's so psychological. The cold doesn't matter. The hunger doesn't matter. The thirst doesn't matter. It's this unbelievable terror that matters, where you just don't know what's going to happen to you next second.

speaker: interviewee
text: You really didn't know, because you may have been perfectly all right and walking next to a barrack, and the German would come and-- and hit you with a whip. And it might hit you on the head and you're gone, or your eyes are gone, and then you're gone, of course. You just didn't know which second is going to bring what. So anyway, that was the first morning. As I said, the hunger-- yeah, of course we were awfully hungry. But that I won't even talk about, because to me, that's secondary. That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: --not that it doesn't matter. I mean, that's ridiculous. Of course it matters. But it goes. It's hunger. So what? Other people have been hungry. There's nothing to it. But the terror-- this unbelievable terror stays.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the next thing that I remember as an outstanding event, in the-- we didn't work, by the way. We were mostly in our barracks. But every so often, we were let out, of course. Once a week we could have a a wash in some cold water that [INAUDIBLE]. Once a week could have a wash. But mostly we were in our barracks. And we were sitting in-- now, the barracks had, again, no windows. But the way it was built, let's say this is the wall, this is the side, and this is the top. And in between the top and the side wall there was a wide [INAUDIBLE] crack. It wasn't a window, but it was definitely a crack. So if it was raining badly, the rain would-- it was big enough for the rain to come in. Anyway, we were sitting up top, and opposite me on the other side-- and, of course, when I say the opposite me, that was so near, because the bunks would be here. In the middle there was something that looked like a heating apparatus, some sort of thing-- which was never heated, as you can imagine. But that would been about that wide. And then the next lot of of bunks were, again, only about that far away. So it was terribly near.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sitting on ours, and suddenly, the most unusual thing happened. One of the Aufseherinnen, the German woman SS guards, came in. Now, she was a blonde woman, quite beautiful. And it is important for me to say that, because again, the incongruity of what-- what is about to be told. There is this very, very beautiful blonde German, whom we've seen, and we knew that she was one of the most cruel people that you could ever come across. Do you know her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. Unfortunately, I have no idea of her name, but-- well, no, I don't. I don't. Even though think I knew, but I think I thought-- but I certainly don't know now, so I take it I've forgotten, because I think I knew the name. Anyway, she was quite-- as I said, she was a very well-known figure around the camp. She always walked with a whip, naturally. And she also walked with a huge dog. And she did some dreadful things. Anyway, she came in. Well, everyone froze, because they never-- the German SS never came in, to the best of my knowledge. Well, not into ours, anyway. She came in and she looked around, and we didn't know what she was going to do. And she did have an SS man behind her. She didn't come alone. She had somebody else behind her. And then, as I said, she looked around. And as she marched up, not on my side of this heating apparatus, [POINTING] but on the other, but came up-- not absolutely opposite, but one up. So it was so near, it would have just about been on my bunk. She went up there, she stood, she looked around, climbed up the beds. And there was a girl sitting up there minding her own business next to the crack. And this Aufseherinnen took her whip and-- --laid into her. She-- well, the screams, I can't tell you, and the blood. And she fell. She fell down. And the Aufseherinnen person, who's done a good job, walked out. What had happened was that this girl was drinking coffee out of her mug, and there was a little bit left, and she threw it out this crack. And the Aufseherinnen was walking by and saw the coffee being poured out, so she made a note of where it was, she came, found the bunk, and that was the end of the girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she died. She died. So that's another scene that you just cannot-- cannot forget. She just walked out as if that was good. My work done. That's fine. Let's go and look for the next one. So that stands out in my mind. The other thing that stands out in this particular lager, when the Gypsy-- Gypsy compound was emptied, and that was one night. We had heard that something is brewing. But we didn't know what was brewing. And then one night we woke up with-- I don't know, anyway. I'll leave it, woke up-- this unbelievable screaming. And we looked out on our slits, and the Gypsy camp, which was right next door to us, the other side of the barbed wire, on one spot was the Gypsy camp. And there were reflectors trained onto the camp from everywhere so that it-- it was like Packer's Cricket Ground-- completely and utterly [INAUDIBLE]. But even that in itself was unbelievably frightening, these huge things, lights coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we could see, of course, people milling around in the light, screaming-- the lot of them screaming. What was happening? They were emptying-- emptying the camp. And the Gypsies knew. So they lined up, x number each time, took them to the gas chambers, finished, back, next lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they knew. They emptied the whole lot. Right through the night it went on. And these people were screaming on the top of their voices all night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we didn't know whether we were the next-- whether we're coming next day, or, you know, what's happening? Are they-- are they-- are they clearing the camp? Well, they weren't. But [INAUDIBLE]. So again, this unbelievable sound in your ears, and the sight of these people running around like little ants there, trying to get away. Of course, nobody can get away. But who [INAUDIBLE]. So that was frightful. It took all night, and then by the morning they were all gone. And there was-- and there was quiet. And we don't know why they decided to kill them all. We have no idea, because nobody else came. As far as I remember, it wasn't filled up. So they were gone. Another-- Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dear me, how can I reconstruct that? Well, it would have had to have been the second month, because after-- it was the third month that we were shifted out from that particular lager. So it would have been sometime in August, I would say-- I would say. So but the dates were very hard, because you didn't really-- although we did know-- when knew when Hitler-- when the attempt on Hitler's life was, July the 20th, we knew that. We heard. God knows how. We knew that there's been an attempt on Hitler's life. And, of course, the first story went around that he's dead. But, of course, he wasn't. So anyway, it was sometime in August, I would say, because it was still summer-- definitely summer, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next thing that stands in my memory is another friend of my mother's, whom we knew very well, who actually committed suicide, or tried, poor thing, when the Germans came in, and somebody saved her. And so she was there in the camp, as well. And she was in another-- not in the same barrack. That she was in barrack number 30, and somebody in number 30 got typhus. So they took the whole thousand people from that-- there were thousand [INAUDIBLE]-- into the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as she was-- we knew that. And as they were walking, she walked in front of us. And she knew, of course, and just looked back and said, goodbye. And that was-- that was that. So I do remember that very well, that terrible, sad look in her face. I'm going. And she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did we get out of that particular lager? And this is the second miracle. There was a transport-- well, because there were-- there where selections all the time. And this particular selection, we had to undress. Mostly you had to undress. In this particular selection, we certainly had to undress. And only very few healthy, good-looking young women were picked. And my sister and I were one of them. Where was the selection done?

speaker: interviewee
text: In front of the barrack somewhere, where you stand [INAUDIBLE] appel outside. So we were selected. Had no idea what for. And we were-- well, first of all, we all had to hang around for a long time before something happened. Anyway, eventually we were taken out of Lager C, and we were taken somewhere. We were told that we're going to be put on the train straightaway. Well, we weren't. And apparently something happened to the-- I don't know. Train wasn't available, whatever. So they said that for all the night, we're going to go over to another lager. And that-- again, I've got to say that my memory is slightly different from my sister's, but I'm going to stick to mine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were taken into another compound, and we were set down on the ground there, waiting. And women were coming down looking for relatives. And whenever someone came in, people were always looked, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these women came down looking for relatives, and some- someone asked names. Who are you all? And we all said our names. And of course, they were all-- there was always, have you met such and such? She looks such and such. Do you remember her? That was constant. Has anyone seen anyone? And we gave our name, and that-- that's it. The person went away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then not too long after that we saw a figure running down this camp street calling our name. It was my mother. Someone had heard our name, being Scheffer, and my mother was the camp doctor, so her name was known, and went up to her and said, I think your daughters are there. There are two girls there called Scheffer. And we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what can I say? Again, we didn't know whether she was alive or dead, although my sister says that we knew. I can't remember that I knew. But maybe I did. But anyway, that's hazy. As I remember it, it was an absolute surprise. We didn't know where she was, alive or dead. And she then replaced us with two other girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: From the transport. She didn't want us to go. How did she manage to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, names didn't matter, you see. It was just two girls who wanted to get out of Auschwitz took our place. The transport was a beautiful girl transport going to the Russian front for the German SS. Can you imagine?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my recollection is that she did. My sister's recollection is that she definitely didn't. So the answer is, I don't know. If she did know, I still understand what she did. I do understand. If she didn't know, well, then there's nothing to understand. And be all that as it may-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --no one came back of that transport. When I say no one, why I can say that, because having-- our transport having been very recent, we'd only just arrived a few weeks or two months ago, we were still in a good condition. So the girls, many of the girls, were from our own transport, because we were in, as I said, good condition still. All very young, and we had stood up to the ravages pretty well at that state. So that's why I know, that there were about 12 girls there, if I remember. At least eight were from my-- my transport, my town, Pecs. None of them came back. And I don't know whether you've ever read which is concerned with that particular aspect of girls for SS soldiers. And in all my many, many books that I've read, because I'm sort of obsessed with the subject, never have I read anything like Well, I call that a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those transports called beautiful--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful girl transports. Uh-huh, yeah. Yeah, specifically for the soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know how often they went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fairly often, because the death rate was so incredibly high and quick that constant, constant replenishment was needed. So quite often. But that was the only-- the first one that we-- we had known, and the first one that we actually got into.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we stayed in this lager where my mother was. And the life there was exactly the same as anywhere else. There was no difference there.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very well thought of. She did everything she could, which was very little. But I think it was more a personality thing, that she did care and she did try. So she was well loved. And there was a book, actually, written after the war in which she is one of the main protagonists, you know, about that particular part of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was she looking after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the lot of them, the sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which, sick? Jewish sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish sick, oh yes, yes. There was a hospital compound there. And when I say hospital, that's-- that's-- that's -- that's blasphemy, because obviously it wasn't a hospital. But it was still somewhere where people were taken in. And my mother did what she could, which was next to nothing. But as I said, it was a personality thing. At least someone to say two nice words, and some reassurance and whatever. But anyway, she was very much liked, that's true, and very, very admired. And that's good. It's a good feeling. It's a good thing to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she ever tell you if they had any kind of medical supplies in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had some [INAUDIBLE]. And yes, there were some, but less and less, and nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The illnesses and injuries were so horrendous that what could you do? But at least-- at least something. So she was the camp doctor there, anyway. She was that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then comes the next miracle-- oh, no. In the meantime, we had a note from my father. We found out that he's in Auschwitz. And he chose to come to Auschwitz because he knew that our area was emptied out and taken to Auschwitz. So he wanted to come to Auschwitz. And the note said, don't worry, we'll be-- this will be over, and we'll be soon together. I think he was dead within two, three weeks after that, because we understand that he died in August. He was gassed, because he had-- he became edemic in the legs, which meant that he couldn't walk very easily, and so he was gassed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- but we didn't know that. We didn't know that he died then. We were quite sure that he's alive. Having recieved--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not until-- literally, not until the end of the war, because although we heard some stories, but you don't believe it, no, until-- until it actually is proven by absence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My idea was, right through, we've got to get out of here. Now, it is true that to get out of Auschwitz was a huge gamble, because you didn't know whether you were getting out of Auschwitz or whether you were taken straight into the gas chamber. But I figured that to stay there is certain death, because you can only survive that long by luck. Luck runs out. Plus time was passing, and the Russians were-- we knew that the Russian front is slowly coming nearer, and we knew that we must be in the way of the Russians, so the Russians were-- and my idea was, and I wasn't far out, that when the Russians come, they'll kill everyone. Well, they didn't kill everybody, because that wasn't possible. But, of course, as good as. So my thing was to get out as soon as-- given opportunity, get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll break up for the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape three of the interview with Maria Scheffer. It's the 21st of July, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.
